content.problemStatement:
Chef got into a fight with the evil Dr Doof. Dr Doof has decided to destroy all even numbers from the universe using his Evil-Destroy-inator. Chef has NNN integers with him. To stop Doof, Chef has to find an odd number which is an integer multiple of all NNN numbers that he has with him. Find if it is possible for Chef to prevent Dr Doof from destroying the even numbers.

Formally, given NNN positive integers, find if there exists an odd number which is an integer multiple of all the given NNN numbers. If yes, print **"YES"**, otherwise **"NO"**. You can print any letter in any case.

###Input


First line contains TTT, number of testcases. Each testcase consists of 222 lines.
The first line of each test case consists of a positive integer NNN, denoting the number of positive integers Chef has.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space separated integers AiA_iAi​ each denoting an integer that Chef has with him.
###Output

For every test case, if there exists such an odd number, print **"YES"** on a separate line, otherwise **"NO"**. The judge is case insensitive. That means, your code can print any letter in any case ( **"Yes"**, **"yes"** or **"YES"** are all accepted).

###Constraints


1≤T≤1031 \leq T \leq 10^31≤T≤103
1≤N≤1031 \leq N \leq 10^31≤N≤103
1≤Ai≤1031 \leq A_i \leq 10^31≤Ai​≤103
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains an integer N, the number of integers Chef has. The second line contains N space‑separated positive integers A1, A2, ..., AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if there exists an odd integer that is a multiple of all given numbers, otherwise output "NO". The case of the letters does not matter.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1 contains even numbers (2 and 4), so the LCM is even and no odd multiple exists → NO. Test case 2 has only the odd number 7, so any odd multiple of 7 works (e.g., 7, 21, 35) → YES.
All three numbers are odd, therefore their LCM is odd. An odd multiple (the LCM itself, 315) exists → YES.
Every number is even, so any common multiple is even. No odd multiple can satisfy the condition → NO.

hidden.label: general case all odd powers of five
general case with a single even number

solution.approach: The least common multiple (LCM) of the given numbers is the smallest positive integer that is a multiple of every Ai. Any multiple of the LCM will also be a multiple of all Ai. Therefore an odd multiple exists iff the LCM itself is odd. The LCM contains a factor 2 exactly when at least one of the Ai is even. Consequently, the answer is "YES" if and only if every Ai is odd; otherwise it is "NO". The algorithm simply scans the list and checks the parity of each number.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the number of integers in that test case. Overall O(T * N_max).
